title: Rib and Chest Wall Musculoskeletal Pain
synonyms: Rib and Chest Wall Musculoskeletal Pain

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Rib and chest wall pain may be benign and mechanical, but chest pain, breathlessness, trauma severity, and systemic features must always be screened carefully before treating it as a simple musculoskeletal problem.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Localised pain with movement, rolling, coughing, or deep breathing
- Point tenderness over rib or intercostal area
- Reduced trunk rotation or protective splinting
- Possible bruising after cough, lift, or minor trauma

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider pneumothorax, pulmonary embolus, ACS, pneumonia, rib fracture, or abdominal referral if the story is atypical
- Breathlessness, hypoxia, syncope, or significant trauma increase urgency
- Osteoporotic or coughing-related rib fractures can occur without major trauma

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen vital signs, trauma history, respiratory symptoms, and red flags before labeling the pain mechanical
- Encourage splinted cough and comfortable breathing where needed
- Escalate for trauma, breathlessness, hypoxia, fever, or chest pain concerning for non-MSK causes

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused chest wall and thoracic examination only when safe and appropriate
- Use breathing, posture, and movement advice once serious pathology is not suspected
- Coordinate medical review or imaging when fracture, respiratory complication, or unexplained severe pain remains possible

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The key decision is whether the pain is truly chest wall pain or a mimic of more serious thoracic disease
